title: Meet Veronika: The Tool-Using Cow That's Blowing Scientists' Minds
summary:
When you think of tool use in animals, you probably picture chimpanzees using sticks to fish for termites, or crows dropping nuts on roads for cars to crack open. But a cow? That's a new one.

Meet Veronika, a Brown Swiss cow who lives on a small farm in Austria. She's the first cow ever reported to use a tool, and her clever trick is making scientists rethink what they know about animal intelligence.

Here's how it works: when Veronika feels an itch she can't reach, she picks up a long-handled deck brush with her tongue. Then she twists her neck to maneuver the brush to exactly the right spot. But the really astonishing part is her decision-making. Scientists from the Messerli Research Institute at the University of Veterinary Medicine in Vienna studied Veronika's technique by placing the brush in different positions. They discovered that she chooses which end of the brush to use based on which body part is itchy. For tough, thick skin on her back, she uses the hard bristles. But for sensitive areas like her belly button or udder, she slowly uses the soft handle instead.

"At the beginning, I thought that it was a mistake when she used the handle," said Antonio Osuna-Mascaró, the lead researcher. "But after observing Veronika for a little while, it was so obvious that she was using both tool ends in different ways."

Sometimes, Veronika even picks up the brush from the middle of the handle. That way, she can just turn her head side to side to use either end — a move that one scientist called "very efficient."

Using the same tool in different ways is something scientists had only seen before in chimpanzees. But Veronika shows that such cleverness isn't limited to our closest relatives. "People are happy to acknowledge that dolphins and so on are extremely clever," said Lindsay Matthews, an animal behavior expert at the University of Auckland. "But I believe most animals are extremely clever if they're given the right task."

Of course, Veronika is just one cow. Not all cows can do this. But her quiet life on a small farm might have given her the freedom to explore and learn new skills. So next time you see a cow grazing in a field, remember — there might be more going on inside that head than you think.
why_it_matters: This challenges conventional views of animal intelligence and suggests that many species may have cognitive abilities that go unnoticed, potentially influencing how we treat farm animals.

keywords.explanation: A Brown Swiss cow who used a brush as a tool, surprising scientists.
An object used to perform a task; Veronika used a deck brush as a tool.
The long-handled deck brush that Veronika manipulated.
The soft part of the brush used for sensitive areas.
The hard part of the brush used for tough spots like her back.
The institute at the University of Veterinary Medicine in Vienna that studied Veronika.

questions.question: How does Veronika pick up the brush?
What does Veronika use for tough spots like her back?
What did one scientist call Veronika's way of picking up the brush from the middle?
Which animal besides Veronika has been seen using the same tool in different ways?

background_read: Tool use was once considered a uniquely human trait, but observations of chimpanzees, orangutans, and capuchin monkeys have shown otherwise.
In recent decades, tool use has been documented in birds like crows and parrots, and even in octopuses.
Cows, however, were not known to use tools.
This study from the Messerli Research Institute at the University of Veterinary Medicine, Vienna, challenges assumptions about ungulate cognition.
The researchers suggest that farm environments may give animals opportunities to develop such skills.
Article_Structure: LEAD: When you think of tool use, you think of chimpanzees or crows, not cows.
└─ But Veronika the cow is changing that.
KEY EVENT: Veronika, a Brown Swiss cow in Austria, picks up a deck brush with her tongue to scratch herself.
├─ WHO: Scientists from the Messerli Research Institute at the University of Veterinary Medicine in Vienna, led by Antonio Osuna-Mascaró
├─ HOW: She twists her neck to maneuver the brush and chooses which end to use based on the itch location.
└─ EVIDENCE: For tough spots like her back, she uses the hard bristles; for sensitive areas, the soft handle.
DISCOVERY: Sometimes she picks up the brush from the middle to efficiently switch ends.
└─ This kind of flexible tool use was previously only seen in chimpanzees.
PERSPECTIVE: Lindsay Matthews from the University of Auckland said most animals are clever if given the right task.
OPEN QUESTION: Is Veronika unique, or can other cows learn this? The study suggests farm environments may allow such behaviors.

perspectives.description: This expands the known range of tool-using species and challenges anthropocentric biases. It encourages studying farm animals in enriched environments.
This is a single case; it might be a learned trick rather than general intelligence. More studies are needed to confirm if other cows can do this.
The finding aligns with growing evidence that many animals, not just primates, have flexible problem-solving skills. It raises questions about how cognitive abilities evolve in domesticated species.
If cows are smarter than we thought, this could lead to improved living conditions on farms. Enrichment activities like providing tools might become standard.
